channel.title: Bull & Bear With Me
channel.description: Markets, explained without the finance-bro energy. A daily 4-minute read for regular people.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 14, 2026
item.description:
**📈 The Open**
Markets are up, oil is down, Goldman is printing money, and somehow the word "ceasefire" is doing more for your 401(k) than your 401(k) manager ever has.
---
**🎯 Big Three**
- **The S&P 500 just erased all its war losses.** We went from "holy hell, missiles" to fully recovered in what felt like a long weekend. Trump signaled he's open to talks with Iran, oil dropped back under $100, and markets did what markets do: immediately assumed the best possible outcome. We're not there yet, but hope is apparently worth about 1% to the S&P.
- **Goldman Sachs made an almost obscene amount of money last quarter.** Profit up 19%. Investment banking fees up 48%. Equities revenue up 27%. Goldman is the friend who thrives during chaos — war, uncertainty, M&A panic — and then smoothly orders another round. The good news: strong Goldman earnings usually mean earnings season broadly is going okay. We'll find out fast — JPMorgan, Citi, and Wells Fargo all report today.
- **Bitcoin ripped 5% and Ethereum had an even better day at nearly 9%.** War optimism lifted all risk assets, and crypto came along for the ride. Worth knowing: Bitcoin is now basically a mood ring for rate expectations. If the Iran situation drags on, inflation stays high, the Fed stays frozen, and that's historically bad for crypto. If things cool down? Different story. Nobody knows. That includes the analysts.
---
**🧠 One Thing Worth Understanding**
**Why does oil dropping matter so much to everything else?**
When oil gets expensive, it costs more to make stuff, ship stuff, and heat your house — which pushes inflation up. When inflation goes up, the Fed raises rates to fight it, which makes borrowing more expensive for everyone: businesses, homebuyers, people buying cars. Higher rates also make "safe" investments like bonds more attractive, so money moves out of stocks and crypto and into bonds. It's a whole domino chain that starts at the gas pump. Right now, oil briefly hit $100 a barrel during the worst of the Iran conflict. It's back at $96 today and falling. That's still not cheap, but the direction matters. Markets aren't reacting to where oil is — they're reacting to where it's going.
---
**👀 Keep An Eye On**
- **Big bank earnings today.** JPMorgan, Wells Fargo, Citi, and Johnson & Johnson all report before the bell. Goldman set a high bar. If the banks disappoint, this week's rally gets tested fast.
- **The Fed's April 28th meeting is creeping up.** Inflation at 3.3%, jobs still solid, war still happening — the Fed is basically a guy at a four-way stop, waving everyone else through. Rate cut odds are near zero. Watch for any Fed speaker comments that change that math.
---
**☕ The Close**
Morgan Stanley just launched the cheapest Bitcoin ETF on Wall Street, which means Bitcoin is now officially a mainstream financial product sold by the same people who charge you to lose money in a target-date fund. We've come so far.
See you tomorrow.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 13, 2026
item.description:
**📈 The Open**
The U.S. just blockaded the Strait of Hormuz, oil hit $104 a barrel, inflation jumped to 3.3%, Goldman missed on bonds, and somehow the S&P is basically flat. Markets are fine. Everything is fine.
---
**🎯 Big Three**
- **The Strait of Hormuz is blockaded.** About 20% of the world's oil flows through that narrow waterway, and we just put a Navy cork in it. Gas was already $4.12 a gallon before this. Analysts are calling it the worst energy disruption since the 1970s, which is a hell of a thing to read before your Monday morning coffee.
- **Inflation jumped from 2.4% to 3.3% in one month.** That's not a drift. That's a lurch. The Fed has two jobs — keep inflation down and keep employment up — and right now both are quietly going sideways. Their response, per Vice Chair Jefferson: our policy is "well-positioned." Comforting stuff, truly.
- **Goldman Sachs had a near-record quarter and still dropped 4%.** Revenues up 14%. EPS beat. But the bond desk missed by $850 million and Wall Street punished them like they showed up to the company picnic without potato salad. The rest of the big banks — JPMorgan, Wells Fargo, Citi, BofA — report this week. Could be the most expensive week of earnings season to actually pay attention to.
---
**🧠 One Thing Worth Understanding**
**The Strait of Hormuz, and why it matters to your drive to work.**
The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow strip of water between Iran and Oman — about 21 miles wide at its tightest point. Something like 20-21% of all the world's oil passes through it every single day, tankers carrying crude from Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Kuwait, and the UAE to the rest of the planet. When that flow gets disrupted — whether by conflict, blockade, or threats — oil prices spike globally because traders don't wait around to see how it plays out. They panic-buy futures and the price jumps immediately, which is why WTI crude went up 8% in one morning. That feeds into gas prices within days, then into shipping costs, then into basically everything that gets transported or made with energy, which is everything. The 1970s oil crisis caused recessions across the Western world. We're not there yet. But the direction of travel is not great, Bob.
---
**👀 Keep An Eye On**
- **Bank earnings all week.** JPMorgan reports Tuesday and tends to set the tone. If they miss, expect the "soft landing" crowd to go quiet for a few days. - **Oil prices Monday night and Tuesday.** Any further escalation around the blockade could push WTI toward $110. That's the number that really starts showing up in everything.
---
**☕ The Close**
Markets are "pricing in the geopolitical risk," which is analyst-speak for "we have no idea what happens next, but we've agreed to be calm about it." Respect the process.
